 {"id":"process_triage-e4y","title":"Add console styling system tests","description":"## Purpose
Create tests to verify the console styling system works correctly across different terminal environments.

## Parent Epic
Console Output Styling Enhancement (process_triage-y8e)

## Depends On
- Add TTY detection and NO_COLOR support (process_triage-18z)
- Implement log_* functions with emoji prefixes (process_triage-vpb)
- Add ANSI fallback when gum unavailable (process_triage-ch7)

## Why This Is Important
The styling system has multiple code paths:
1. TTY with gum → full styling
2. TTY without gum → ANSI fallback
3. Non-TTY (pipe) → no styling
4. NO_COLOR set → no colors
5. CI environment → minimal styling

Each path must be tested to ensure correct behavior.

## Test Scenarios

### test/test_console_styling.bats

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bats

load 'test_helper/common'

setup() {
    setup_test_env
    test_start "$BATS_TEST_NAME" "Console styling test"
    export PT_SCRIPT="${BATS_TEST_DIRNAME}/../pt"
}

teardown() {
    test_end "$BATS_TEST_NAME" "${BATS_TEST_COMPLETED:-fail}"
    restore_path
    teardown_test_env
}

#==============================================================================
# TTY DETECTION TESTS
#==============================================================================

@test "Styling: detects TTY correctly" {
    test_info "Testing TTY detection"
    
    # When run directly (not piped), should detect TTY
    # This is tricky to test because BATS output is piped
    
    # Test the detection logic directly by sourcing
    source "$PT_SCRIPT" 2>/dev/null || true
    
    test_info "IS_TTY should be set based on terminal"
    
    BATS_TEST_COMPLETED=pass
}

@test "Styling: piped output disables colors" {
    test_info "Testing piped output detection"
    
    create_mock_ps "$(mock_ps_with_stuck_test)"
    use_mock_bin
    
    # Pipe through cat to simulate non-TTY
    output=$("$PT_SCRIPT" scan 2>&1 | cat)
    
    test_info "Checking for absence of escape codes in piped output"
    
    # Should NOT contain ANSI escape codes when piped
    # Note: escape code is \033 or \e
    if [[ "$output" == *$'\033'* ]]; then
        test_warn "Found escape codes in piped output"
        # This might be acceptable if stderr is still TTY
    fi
    
    BATS_TEST_COMPLETED=pass
}

#==============================================================================
# NO_COLOR SUPPORT TESTS
#==============================================================================

@test "Styling: NO_COLOR=1 disables all colors" {
    test_info "Testing NO_COLOR support"
    
    export NO_COLOR=1
    create_mock_ps "$(mock_ps_with_stuck_test)"
    use_mock_bin
    
    test_info "Running pt scan with NO_COLOR=1"
    run "$PT_SCRIPT" scan
    
    test_info "Output: $output"
    
    # Should NOT contain ANSI escape sequences
    assert_not_contains "$output" $'\033[' "Should not contain ANSI codes"
    
    BATS_TEST_COMPLETED=pass
}

@test "Styling: NO_COLOR=0 still disables colors (presence matters, not value)" {
    test_info "Testing NO_COLOR=0 (any value disables)"
    
    # Per no-color.org spec, presence of variable matters, not value
    export NO_COLOR=0
    create_mock_ps "$(mock_ps_with_stuck_test)"
    use_mock_bin
    
    run "$PT_SCRIPT" scan
    
    # Should still be disabled (NO_COLOR is set)
    # This depends on implementation following the spec
    
    BATS_TEST_COMPLETED=pass
}

@test "Styling: unset NO_COLOR allows colors" {
    test_info "Testing without NO_COLOR"
    
    unset NO_COLOR
    create_mock_ps "$(mock_ps_with_stuck_test)"
    use_mock_bin
    
    # This test is tricky because BATS captures output
    # Colors would appear in TTY but not in captured output
    
    test_info "Running without NO_COLOR"
    run "$PT_SCRIPT" scan
    
    BATS_TEST_COMPLETED=pass
}

#==============================================================================
# CI ENVIRONMENT TESTS
#==============================================================================

@test "Styling: CI=true modifies behavior" {
    test_info "Testing CI environment detection"
    
    export CI=true
    create_mock_ps "$(mock_ps_with_stuck_test)"
    use_mock_bin
    
    test_info "Running pt scan with CI=true"
    run "$PT_SCRIPT" scan
    
    # In CI, should work without interactive elements
    assert_equals "0" "$status" "Should succeed in CI"
    
    BATS_TEST_COMPLETED=pass
}

@test "Styling: GITHUB_ACTIONS=true detected as CI" {
    test_info "Testing GitHub Actions detection"
    
    export GITHUB_ACTIONS=true
    create_mock_ps "$(mock_ps_with_stuck_test)"
    use_mock_bin
    
    run "$PT_SCRIPT" scan
    
    assert_equals "0" "$status" "Should succeed in GitHub Actions"
    
    BATS_TEST_COMPLETED=pass
}

#==============================================================================
# GUM AVAILABILITY TESTS
#==============================================================================

@test "Styling: works without gum installed" {
    test_info "Testing fallback when gum unavailable"
    
    # Hide gum from PATH
    mkdir -p "${MOCK_BIN}"
    cat > "${MOCK_BIN}/gum" << 'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
exit 127  # Command not found
EOF
    chmod +x "${MOCK_BIN}/gum"
    
    # Also remove real gum from PATH
    export PATH="${MOCK_BIN}:/usr/bin:/bin"
    
    create_mock_ps "$(mock_ps_with_stuck_test)"
    
    test_info "Running pt scan without gum"
    run "$PT_SCRIPT" scan
    
    # Should still work using ANSI fallback
    # Might show warning about gum
    
    BATS_TEST_COMPLETED=pass
}

@test "Styling: gum_confirm falls back to read" {
    test_info "Testing gum_confirm fallback"
    
    # This tests the wrapper function directly
    # Hide gum
    export PATH="/usr/bin:/bin"
    export GUM_AVAILABLE=false
    
    # The fallback should use read for input
    # This is hard to test automatically
    
    BATS_TEST_COMPLETED=pass
}

#==============================================================================
# LOG FUNCTION TESTS
#==============================================================================

@test "Styling: log_info outputs to stderr" {
    test_info "Testing log_info output stream"
    
    # Source the script to get functions
    source "$PT_SCRIPT" 2>/dev/null || true
    
    # If log_info is available, test it
    if declare -f log_info >/dev/null 2>&1; then
        # Capture stderr separately
        local stderr_output
        stderr_output=$(log_info "test message" 2>&1 >/dev/null)
        
        test_info "stderr output: $stderr_output"
        assert_contains "$stderr_output" "test message" "Should output to stderr"
    else
        test_warn "log_info not yet implemented"
    fi
    
    BATS_TEST_COMPLETED=pass
}

@test "Styling: log_error includes error indicator" {
    test_info "Testing log_error formatting"
    
    source "$PT_SCRIPT" 2>/dev/null || true
    
    if declare -f log_error >/dev/null 2>&1; then
        local output
        output=$(log_error "test error" 2>&1)
        
        # Should include error indicator (✗ or [ERROR] etc)
        test_info "Error output: $output"
    fi
    
    BATS_TEST_COMPLETED=pass
}

@test "Styling: log_success includes success indicator" {
    test_info "Testing log_success formatting"
    
    source "$PT_SCRIPT" 2>/dev/null || true
    
    if declare -f log_success >/dev/null 2>&1; then
        local output
        output=$(log_success "test success" 2>&1)
        
        # Should include success indicator (✓ or [OK] etc)
        test_info "Success output: $output"
    fi
    
    BATS_TEST_COMPLETED=pass
}

#==============================================================================
# COLOR CODE TESTS
#==============================================================================

@test "Styling: color variables defined when USE_COLOR=true" {
    test_info "Testing color variable definitions"
    
    unset NO_COLOR
    export USE_COLOR=true
    
    source "$PT_SCRIPT" 2>/dev/null || true
    
    # Check color variables are set
    if [[ -n "${RED:-}" ]]; then
        test_info "RED is defined: ${RED@Q}"
        # Should contain escape code
        [[ "$RED" == *"033"* ]] || [[ "$RED" == *"e["* ]]
    fi
    
    BATS_TEST_COMPLETED=pass
}

@test "Styling: color variables empty when USE_COLOR=false" {
    test_info "Testing color variables disabled"
    
    export NO_COLOR=1
    
    source "$PT_SCRIPT" 2>/dev/null || true
    
    # Color variables should be empty
    if [[ -z "${RED:-}" ]] || [[ "$RED" == "" ]]; then
        test_info "RED is correctly empty when colors disabled"
    fi
    
    BATS_TEST_COMPLETED=pass
}
```

## Success Criteria
- [ ] TTY detection tested
- [ ] Piped output tested
- [ ] NO_COLOR support tested (various values)
- [ ] CI environment detection tested
- [ ] Gum availability/fallback tested
- [ ] Log functions tested (stderr, formatting)
- [ ] Color variable states tested
- [ ] All tests have detailed logging

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Implements the full scope described above (no silent omissions).
- [ ] Handles edge/error cases described above with deterministic behavior.
- [ ] Includes unit tests for the core logic and the highest-risk edge cases.
- [ ] Emits structured logs/telemetry for key decisions and failures (as applicable).

## Test Plan
- Unit: golden tests for core logic; edge cases and error paths.
- Integration: exercise real tool/system interactions where applicable (or mocked equivalents).
- E2E: cover at least one representative user/agent workflow when applicable.
- Logging: tests validate presence/shape of critical log/telemetry fields.

 {"id":"process_triage-ed3","title":"EPIC: Phase 11 - Pattern and Signature Library","description":"## Overview
Phase 11 builds a signature library for known process types with pre-calibrated priors and specialized detection logic.

## Background
The plan specifies bundled signatures for common processes: test runners, dev servers, build tools, databases, etc. Each signature includes characteristic patterns, expected lifetimes, typical resource usage, and calibrated Bayesian priors.

## Why It Matters
Generic priors work but specialized priors are much more accurate. A signature for 'jest test runner' knows that tests typically complete in minutes, not hours, and that high CPU during execution is normal. This domain knowledge dramatically improves inference accuracy.

## Phase Scope
1. Signature schema definition
2. Core signature library (50+ common processes)
3. Signature matching engine
4. Prior override system
5. Community signature format

## Signature Schema
- pattern: Regex matching command/exe
- category: test_runner|dev_server|build_tool|database|...
- expected_lifetime: {min, typical, max} in seconds
- resource_profile: {cpu: normal_range, memory: normal_range}
- priors: {abandoned: Beta(a,b), zombie: Beta(a,b), ...}
- detection_hints: Special signals for this process type
- kill_safety: {safe_to_term: bool, needs_drain: bool, ...}

## Bundled Signatures (Examples)
- Test runners: jest, pytest, bats, go test, cargo test
- Dev servers: next dev, vite, webpack-dev-server, rails s
- Build tools: make, cargo build, npm run build, gcc/clang
- Databases: postgres, mysql, redis, mongodb
- Agent shells: claude, codex, aider processes

## Matching Priority
1. Exact command match
2. Exe path match
3. Argument pattern match
4. Generic category fallback

## Success Criteria
- 50+ signatures for common dev tools
- Signature matching fast and accurate
- Priors improve inference accuracy (measured)
- Community contribution path documented","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-15T08:33:59.015590401Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-15T09:07:09.511765574Z","closed_at":"2026-01-15T09:07:09.511765574Z","close_reason":"Superseded by process_triage-79x (canonical Phase 11 epic).  {"id":"process_triage-ed3.1","title":"Implement user signature customization (signatures.json + add/remove/list)","description":"## Context
Phase 11 (Pattern/Signature Library).

## Problem
Users need to teach pt about their own tools and workflows. A curated library is not enough; we need first-class user customization.

## Requirements
- Config file: `~/.config/process_triage/signatures.json`
- Commands:
  - `pt signature list`
  - `pt signature add` (guided, validates pattern)
  - `pt signature remove`
- Merge behavior:
  - built-in signatures + user signatures; user overrides allowed
- Validation:
  - schema validation
  - test match preview (`--dry-run` match against current processes)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] User signatures load and merge deterministically.
- [ ] CLI commands work and are documented.
- [ ] Invalid signatures fail with actionable errors.

## Test Plan
- Unit tests: merge/override behavior.
- E2E: create user signatures and confirm matches.

-{"id":"process_triage-ed3.2","title":"Implement signature-informed inference fast path (bypass/priors)","description":"## Context
Phase 11.

## Problem
Full inference can be expensive. When a process matches a high-confidence signature (e.g., known dev server pattern), we should be able to:
- skip deep inference
- or use signature-provided priors to accelerate and improve calibration

## Requirements
- Signature match returns a confidence score.
- If confidence ≥ threshold:
  - fast-path classification/prior override
  - still produce an evidence ledger (“matched signature X”)
- Track fast-path usage in telemetry.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] High-confidence matches trigger fast-path behavior.
- [ ] Output remains explainable and auditable.
- [ ] Fast-path can be disabled via config/policy.

## Test Plan
- Unit tests: match confidence thresholds.
- Golden tests: known signature fixtures.

 {"id":"process_triage-ed3.3","title":"Track and calibrate signature match confidence","description":"## Context
Phase 11.

## Problem
Signature confidence scores must be calibrated; otherwise we risk false certainty and unsafe recommendations.

## Approach
- Persist signature match outcomes:
  - match id, confidence score, eventual human decision/outcome
- Compute calibration metrics in shadow mode:
  - reliability curves for match confidence
  - confusion matrix by signature category
- Provide tooling to adjust thresholds and identify bad signatures.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Telemetry contains sufficient fields to analyze signature performance.
- [ ] Produces a calibration report artifact (DuckDB query or HTML section).

## Test Plan
- Unit tests: aggregation and metric computation on fixtures.
","notes":"Implemented: (1) calibrate submodules (metrics.rs, curve.rs, bias.rs, report.rs, queries.rs) with Brier/ECE/AUC-ROC metrics, calibration curves, bias detection, and DuckDB queries; (2) signature_matches telemetry table for calibration data; (3) signature tracking fields in proc_inference schema","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"ClaudeOpus4","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-15T08:56:06.714969685Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-21T21:42:52.642859921Z","closed_at":"2026-01-21T21:42:52.642699017Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"process_triage-ed3.3","depends_on_id":"process_triage-79x","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-02T00:30:58Z","created_by":"import"}]}
 {"id":"process_triage-ed3.4","title":"Support signature sharing via .ptb bundles","description":"## Context
Phase 11.

## Problem
Teams may want to share signature libraries across machines (fleet) or between users.

## Requirements
- Export signatures into `.ptb` bundles (as a profile option).
- Import signatures from a bundle with validation and merge preview.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Bundled signatures can be exported/imported deterministically.
- [ ] Import validates schema and shows diff before applying.

## Test Plan
- E2E: export then import and verify identical match behavior.

 {"id":"process_triage-erz","title":"Implement pt agent snapshot command","description":"## Overview
Implement the `pt agent snapshot` command that captures system state and creates a session for subsequent operations.

## From Plan Section 3.5

### Command Purpose
Returns a session_id and system state snapshot for subsequent agent operations. This is typically the first command in an agent workflow.

### Output Contract
```json
{
  'session_id': 'abc123',
  'host_id': 'devbox1',
  'timestamp': '2025-01-15T10:30:00Z',
  'system_state': {
    'load': [5.17, 4.89, 10.23],
    'cores': 64,
    'memory': {'total_gb': 499, 'used_gb': 281, 'available_gb': 218},
    'process_count': 412,
    'psi': {'cpu': 0.02, 'memory': 0.05, 'io': 0.01}
  },
  'capabilities': {
    'tools': {'perf': true, 'bpftrace': false, 'strace': true, ...},
    'permissions': {'can_sudo': false, 'can_ptrace': true}
  }
}
```

### Session Creation
- Generate unique session_id
- Create artifact directory: `~/.local/share/process_triage/sessions/<session_id>/`
- Persist initial snapshot

### Workflow Integration
```
Agent invocation 1: pt agent snapshot
  → Returns session_id, system state, capabilities
  → Agent decides: 'need to clean up, let me plan'

Agent invocation 2: pt agent plan --session <id>
  → Reuses session context from snapshot
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Command generates unique session_id
- [ ] System state captured accurately
- [ ] Capabilities detected and reported
- [ ] Session artifact directory created
- [ ] JSON output is well-formed
- [ ] --format json/md supported

## Dependencies
- Phase 1 (session model, CLI surface)

## Technical Notes
- System state collection should be fast (< 100ms)
- Capabilities detection uses cached results where available
- Session IDs should be short and URL-safe","notes":"Reviewed agent snapshot implementation (main.rs agent snapshot + session persistence tests in crates/pt-core/tests/e2e_snapshot.rs / e2e_cli_scenarios.rs). Dependencies (t6lf, qje, 3ir) closed; ready to close.","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"EmeraldEagle","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-15T08:53:27.253564573Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-02-04T05:11:20.741474617Z","closed_at":"2026-02-04T05:11:20.741337522Z","close_reason":"Completed: agent snapshot command implemented + tests","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"process_triage-erz","depends_on_id":"process_triage-3ir","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-02T00:30:58Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"process_triage-erz","depends_on_id":"process_triage-bwn","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-02T00:30:58Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"process_triage-erz","depends_on_id":"process_triage-qje","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-02T00:30:58Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"process_triage-erz","depends_on_id":"process_triage-t6lf","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-02T00:30:58Z","created_by":"import"}]}
